coredata.dc:title: Voltammetric determination of ethinylestradiol using screen-printed electrode modified with functionalized graphene, graphene quantum dots and magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Santos, Anderson M.
Wong, Ademar
Prado, Thiago M.
Fava, Elson L.
Fatibello-Filho, Orlando
Sotomayor, Maria D.P.T.
Moraes, Fernando C.
coredata.dc:description: The present work reports the development of a sensitive and selective method for ethinylestradiol detection using screen-printed electrode (SPE) modified with functionalized graphene (FG), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (mag@MIP). The performance of the mag@MIP sensor was compared with that of a non-molecularly imprinted sensor (mag@NIP). Chemical and physical characterizations of the mag@NIP and mag@MIP sensors were performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) techniques. The electrochemical behavior of the electrodes investigated, which included (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE, (mag@NIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE, GQDs-FG-NF/SPE and FG-NF/SPE, was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry. The results obtained show a significant increase in peak current magnitude for (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE. Using square wave voltammetry experiments, the efficiency of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor was also tested under optimized conditions. The linear response range obtained for ethinylestradiol concentration was 10 nmol L−1 to 2.5 μmol L−1, with limit of detection of 2.6 nmol L−1. The analytical signal of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor suffered no interference from different compounds and the sensor exhibited good repeatability. The proposed sensor was successfully applied for ethynilestradiol detection in river water, serum and urine samples, where recovery rates between 96 to 105% and 97–104% were obtained for environmental and biological samples, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIP
Graphene
Quantum dots
Screen-printed electrode
Ethinylestradiol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of functionalized graphene and graphene quantum dots
Preparation and modification of the SPE sensor
Synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers
Preparation of river water, urine and serum samples
Determination of ethinylestradiol in river water, serum and urine samples

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SPE modification with GQDs-FG-NF and (B) Fixing the magnet to the SPE modified.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles (mag) and mag@MIP.
(A) SEM micrograph and (B) corresponding EDX spectrum of the mag (Fe3O4). SEM micrograph of (C–E) mag@NIP and (F–H) mag@MIP.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 420 nm. Inset: UV–Vis absorbance spectra of GQDs and photographs of GQDs solution taken under visible light (white) and UV light (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Sensor mechanism: (A) addition of the analyte; (B) incubation step with the mag@MIP in the analyte solution; (C) analyte adsorption by the mag@MIP; (D) separation of the analyte-mag@MIP with the electrode magnet (GQDs-FG-NF/SPE), and (E) steps of electrochemical analysis.

Cyclic voltammograms obtained for 5.0 μmol L−1 EE2 in 0.2 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution pH 7.0 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for the following electrodes: (a) bare SPE, (b) GR-NF/SPE, (c) FG-NF/SPE, GQDs-FG-NF/SPE, (d) (mag@NIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE and (e) (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE.
Current response as function of the pH for the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor, performed by SWV for 2.0 μmol L−1 EE2 in 0.2 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution. Inset: ΔI p vs. pH and ΔE p vs. pH.

SWV voltammograms recorded at the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE for different concentrations of EE2 (10 nmol L−1 to 2.5 μmol L−1) in 0.2 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution. Inset: corresponding analytical curve. Supporting electrolyte: 0.20 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). Experimental setup: f = 30 Hz, a = 50 mV, ΔE s = 5 mV and t pc 75 s.
Proposed electrochemical reaction for EE2 oxidation.
Comparison between electroanalytical methods for the determination of EE2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Single wall carbon nanotubes decorated with nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Multiwalled carbon nanotubes and magnetic nanoparticles functionalized with tannic acid and Au nanoparticles deposited onto the glassy carbon electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Glassy carbon electrode modified with reduced graphene oxide and Ru nanoparticles.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Palladium Nanoparticles/Graphitic Carbon Nitride Nanosheets-Carbon Nanotubes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon paste electrode in the presence of cetyl pyridine bromide.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results obtained using the proposed SWV method in the determination of EE2 in synthetic human urine, serum and river samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: River A: Rio Gregório – São Carlos/SP; River B: Rio Chibarro – Araraquara/SP.

head.ce:title: Voltammetric determination of ethinylestradiol using screen-printed electrode modified with functionalized graphene, graphene quantum dots and magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of São Carlos, Rod. Washington Luís km 235, P. O. Box 676, 13560-970, São Carlos, SP, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Sao Carlos, Rod. Washington Luis km 235, P. O. Box 676, 13560-970, Sao Carlos, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Institute of Chemistry, State University of São Paulo and INCT-DATREM, 14801-970, Araraquara, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State University of São Paulo and INCT-DATREM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Institute of Chemistry, State University of Sao Paulo and INCT-DATREM, 14801-970 Araraquara, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work reports the development of a sensitive and selective method for ethinylestradiol detection using screen-printed electrode (SPE) modified with functionalized graphene (FG), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (mag@MIP). The performance of the mag@MIP sensor was compared with that of a non-molecularly imprinted sensor (mag@NIP). Chemical and physical characterizations of the mag@NIP and mag@MIP sensors were performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) techniques. The electrochemical behavior of the electrodes investigated, which included (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE, (mag@NIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE, GQDs-FG-NF/SPE and FG-NF/SPE, was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry. The results obtained show a significant increase in peak current magnitude for (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE. Using square wave voltammetry experiments, the efficiency of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor was also tested under optimized conditions. The linear response range obtained for ethinylestradiol concentration was 10 nmol L−1 to 2.5 μmol L−1, with limit of detection of 2.6 nmol L−1. The analytical signal of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor suffered no interference from different compounds and the sensor exhibited good repeatability. The proposed sensor was successfully applied for ethynilestradiol detection in river water, serum and urine samples, where recovery rates between 96 to 105% and 97–104% were obtained for environmental and biological samples, respectively.

Schematic presentation of a screen-printed sensor modified with functionalized graphene, graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) for the determination of ethinylestradiol in biological and environmental samples by voltammetry.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, there have been reports in the literature regarding the contamination of water bodies by endocrine disruptors (EDs) and the imminent risks that this poses to humans and other living beings, as well as to the environment in general [1]. EDs belong to a class of micro pollutants found in surface waters (such as lakes, rivers and streams) and which cause dysfunctions in the endocrine system of aquatic organisms. Some of the major harmful effects caused by EDs include the feminization of species and the resulting decline in reproduction rates [2]. In humans, apart from the aforementioned effects, the presence of EDs in the body may lead to the development of carcinomas and the decrease of sperm production in males [3].

The most commonly reported EDs in risk assessment studies are phthalates [4], agricultural pesticides [5], polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), dioxins, and estrogens [6]. There are different types of estrogens; these include the following: the phytoestrogens, which are plant-derived compounds found in a wide variety of foods (the most abundant of these compounds are the isoflavones present in soybeans) [7]; the xenoestrogens (synthetic estrogens), which interfere in the activity of natural estrogens (e.g. bisphenol-A, PCBs, 17-α-ethynylestradiol (EE2), among other compounds); and the natural estrogens, which are three hormones (including estriol, 17-β-estradiol and estrone) produced by the female organism [8].

EE2 is used in contraceptive pills and in hormone replacement therapy. This hormone is found to be widely consumed, and due to its resistance to degradation, it easily finds its way into domestic sewage, leading to its accumulation in aquatic bodies [9]. In view of that, EE2 is considered one of the major EDs and water contaminants that deserve to be monitored. The quantification of EE2 in water samples is commonly performed by chromatographic methods; these include solid-phase extraction with liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry [10], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [11], and high performance liquid chromatography coupled to UV–vis detector [12] or mass spectrometer [13].

Over the past decades, there have been advances and evolution of methodologies used in the construction of electrochemical sensors. With regard to sensor fabrication, researchers have focused their attention on the development and application of materials with outstanding properties capable of boosting sensor efficiency and performance. These suitable properties include high surface area, high charge transfer rate, specific interactions with target analytes, and so forth [14–17]. When it comes to the detection of EDs in water samples, electroanalytical techniques have become increasingly employed as an alternative strategy for this purpose [18,19]. Electrochemical methods have been shown to present analytical performance that is statistically comparable to that of chromatographic methods, with the former having the advantages of not requiring laborious sample preparation, apart from involving relatively shorter time of analysis and low consumption of reagents [20–22].

Considering the presence of high content of EE2 in surface water samples and the risks it poses to human health, the development of efficient electrochemical sensors for EE2 detection has gained an increasingly considerable attention among researchers. Santos et al. (2016) developed a sensor modified with organic-inorganic composite film using the layer-by-layer technique [23]. Canevari et al. (2016) also employed the hybrid technique for the fabrication of their proposed sensor, where the authors combined laccase enzyme and single-walled carbon nanotubes decorated with carbon quantum dots to enhance the sensor performance; their application of laccase was predicated on its specific ability to oxidize the phenolic group from EE2 [24].

Although the use of biosensors for EE2 detection leads to high selectivity [25–27], the performance of these sensors may be impaired by the instability of the biological material with the variation of pH and temperature. In this sense, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) are employed as an alternative strategy for ensuring the selectivity of the sensors. The MIP is formed by the crosslinking of the monomer around the template molecule, followed by polymerization. After polymerization, the template molecule is removed by extraction, leading to the production of specific sites. The specific sites from the MIP are then able to recognize the template molecule present in samples of interest [28].

The MIP has been employed in a wide range of techniques; these include sorbent in solid-phase extraction [29–31] and in the modification of electrode for electroanalysis [32,33]. In the literature, the application of MIP for the detection of EE2 has only been reported for the solid-phase extraction technique [34–36].

The present work reports the development and application of an innovative technique which involves the use of magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4) coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (mag@MIP) for the selective detection of EE2 in biological and environmental samples, followed by electrochemical analysis. A magnet was used to retain the MIP on the surface of the screen-printed electrode (SPE). Thus, the extraction of EE2 from the samples by the mag@MIP provided the sensor with good selectivity. The modification of the SPE surface with functionalized graphene (FG) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) contributed toward improving the analytical signal of the sensor.

Ethinylestradiol (EE2), Nafion® (NF), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), trimethylolpropane trimethacrylate (TRIM), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2-azobisobutyronitrile (AIBN), methacryloxypropyl trimethoxysilane (MPS), methacrylic acid, acrylonitrile, 2-vinylpyridine, 1-vinylimidazole, FeCl2●4H2O, FeCl3●6H2O and HNO3 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (http://www.sigmaaldrich.com). Citric acid, ethanol, toluene, KCl, KH2PO4, K2HPO4 H2SO4 and NaOH were acquired from Across (https://www.acros.com). Graphene was acquired from Graphene Supermarket (www.graphene-supermarket.com). All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (with resistivity not less than 18 MΩ cm at 25 °C) obtained from a Milli-Q Direct-0.3 (Millipore) purification system (https://www.emdmillipore.com).

The photoluminescence properties of the GQDs were measured using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrofluorophotometer (www.ssi.shimadzu.com). Absorbance (UV/Vis) measurements were performed using a Shimadzu UV-2550 (www.ssi.shimadzu.com) spectrometer with a quartz cuvette and slit width of 2 nm (optical path length of 1 cm).

Morphological characterization of the different materials was performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images obtained from a scanning electron microscope (Model 7500F, JEOL) (www.jeol.co.jp).

Specific surface areas of the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and the non-molecularly imprinted polymer (NIP) were characterized by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, based on N2 adsorption isotherms using a Micromeritics ASAP 2000 Surface Area Analyzer (www.micromeritics.com).
Electrochemical measurements were performed using an Autolab model PGSTAT 128N potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm-Autolab, https://www.metrohm.com) with the aid of the NOVA 2.1 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized graphene and graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Functionalized graphene (FG) was obtained by the addition of 100 mg of graphene into a concentrated solution of 1:1 (v/v) H2SO4/HNO3. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature (25 °C). Thereafter, the FG suspension was filtered and washed with ultrapure water until pH of 7.0 was reached and dried at 100 °C [37].

The synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was conducted based on adaptation from the work published by Naik et al. (2017) [38]. To perform the synthesis, 1 g of citric acid was heated for 30 min until it melted and the color was changed to dark orange. Subsequently, an amount of 50 mL of 0.25 mol L−1 NaOH solution was added to the melted citric acid, leading to the formation of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and modification of the SPE sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The screen-printed electrode (SPE) fabricated consisted of the working electrode (diameter of 3.0 mm), counter electrode and pseudo-reference electrode, all integrated into a single platform. The following items were used for the preparation of the SPE sensor: a Silhouette CAMEO 3 clipping printer, vinyl adhesive sheet, polyester sheet, carbon conductive and Ag/AgCl conductive inks. Initially, the vinyl adhesive was bound to the polyester sheet, and with the aid of the clipping printer, the masks were obtained with the electrode delimitations (previously designed in the Silhouette Studio 4.3 software). Afterwards, the conductive carbon ink was deposited, followed by drying for 30 min at 90 °C. Next, the Ag/AgCl ink was manually deposited to form the reference electrodes; this was followed by drying for 30 min at 60 °C. The vinyl adhesive was finally removed, thus obtaining the electrodes which are to be subjected to modification.

The modification of the SPE was performed under optimized conditions; this involved adding 8 μL of a dispersion of GQDs-FG-NF prepared from 1.0 mg of FG, 10 μL of NF and 990 μL of GQDs (Fig. 1 A). Finally, a magnet was attached behind the working electrode for the subsequent capture and immobilization of the mag@MIP (Fig. 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the magnetic nanoparticles coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (mag@MIP) was performed as previously reported in the literature [39]. Initially, the magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4) were prepared by the co-precipitation method; this involved mixing 4.72 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 1.72 g of FeCl2·4H2O in 80 mL of deionized water, under constant agitation in inert atmosphere (N2). After that, 1.5 mol L−1 NaOH solution was added to the mixture under constant stirring until pH of 10 was reached; this resulted in a dark precipitate, indicating the formation of magnetite. Subsequently, the precipitate (Fe3O4) was washed, separated with the aid of a magnet and dried at room temperature (25 °C). To enable the functionalization of the material for further reaction, the material obtained (Fe3O4) was modified with TEOS, leading to the formation of Fe3O4@SiO2. The procedure involved adding an amount of 300 mg of Fe3O4 in a solution containing 5.0 mL of aqueous ammonia and 40 mL of ethanol; this was followed by the slow addition of 0.75 mL of TEOS and stirring for 12 h. After this procedure, the material was silanized in order to generate activated CC groups. This procedure consisted of mixing 250 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2, 5 mL of MPS and 45 mL of toluene and leaving the mixture under inert atmosphere (N2) for 12 h. The Fe3O4@SiO2-MPS (mag) obtained was washed with ethanol and deionized water and dried at room temperature (25 °C).

Finally, mag@MIP was synthesized by photopolymerization. In this step, 0.2 mmol of EE2 (template molecule) and 8.0 mmol of methacrylic acid (functional monomer) were dispersed in 25 mL of methanol in N2 atmosphere at 25 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, an amount of 200 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2-MPS (mag) was added to the mixture and stirred for 2 h, followed by the addition of 50 mmol of AIBN (radical initiator) and 3.0 mmol of TRIM (cross-linking reagent). Afterwards, the photopolymerization was initiated by putting the resulting mixture in water bath (5 °C), sealing and irradiating it with a high-pressure mercury lamp (125 W) for 2 h.

The mag@MIP obtained was washed several times to remove both the template molecule and the excess of other reagents. Similarly, a non-molecularly imprinted polymer (mag@NIP used as control) was prepared following the same procedure adopted for the mag@MIP, but without the addition of the template molecule (EE2). A summary of the sequence of experiments carried out in the synthesis of mag@MIP is shown in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of river water, urine and serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The river water samples were collected from the Gregório river (River A, 21°59′11.0″S 47°52′52.1″W) and the Chibarro river (River B, 21°48′25.0″S 48°10′21.7″W), located in the cities of São Carlos and Araraquara (São Paulo State, Brazil), respectively. The serum samples were prepared using commercial bovine calf serum purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (http://www.sigmaaldrich.com). Synthetic urine was prepared by adding the following reagents in a volumetric flask: 0.010 g of NH4Cl, 0.012 g of CaCl2, 0.016 g of KCl, 0.016 g of KH2PO4, 0.028 g of NaCl and 0.24 g of urea [40]. Subsequently, the flask volume (10 mL) was filled up with ultrapure water. Next, the river water, serum and urine samples were spiked with EE2 at two concentration levels (0.080 and 1.0 μmol L−1) and analyzed by the proposed method.

The surface morphology analysis of mag (Fe3O4), mag@MIP and mag@NIP was carried out based on micrographies recorded by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 3 A shows a homogeneous agglomeration of mag (Fe3O4) in sizes ranging from 10 to 15 nm, and the corresponding chemical analysis (EDX spectrum) revealed the presence of iron and oxygen belonging to mag (Fe3O4) (Fig. 3B). When the magnetic nanoparticles were coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (core shell), an increase was observed in the size of the particles (150–350 nm). Fig. 3 (B-D) shows the surface of mag@MIP in different magnified images. One can clearly observe the presence of agglomerated particles with spherical appearance of different sizes. Similar images can be observed in Fig. 3 (E-G), where the surface of mag@NIP was analyzed. This result was readily expected considering that these polymers have the same chemical composition. It is worth noting that the formation of selective cavities occurs at molecular level, and the selective cavities of the MIP are not clearly visible. In view of that, to have a better understanding of mag@MIP and mag@NIP, analysis of the surface area of the polymers was carried out using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique. The results showed that the mag@MIP has a relatively greater surface area compared to the mag@NIP; this is probably due to the presence of the cavities selective to EE2 which are found in the mag@MIP. The values obtained for the surface area of the materials were: 31.3 m2 g−1 for mag@MIP and 19.3 m2 g−1 for mag@NIP (Fig. S1 - Supporting Material); these values show the influence of the template molecule on the formation of bigger cavities, which results in relatively greater surface area for the MIP compared to the NIP.

Fluorescence emission spectrophotometry analysis was performed aiming at investigating the formation of GQDs. Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs excited in different wavelengths (λ: 360 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm and 420 nm). Fluorescence emission response occurred at the wavelength of 470 nm; this shows that the GQDs had radius between 2 and 3 nm. Under all the excitation wavelengths applied, the material presented the same response obtained for the emission wavelength; this points to the uniformity of the GQDs; in other words, the nanoparticles exhibited identical luminescent centers [38]. The quantum dots are semiconductor particles with diameter of few nanometers; their optical and electric properties are different from macroscopic semiconductors [41]. The quantum dots have quantized energy with discrete values due to the quantum confinement of their electrons [41]. The size of the quantum dots needs to be changed if one seeks to control their energy level [42]. Thus, for the same kind of compound, larger quantum dots (with radius between 5 and 6 nm) emit light at longer wavelengths, generating colors like orange and red; this is because these larger particles present relatively lower energy of the forbidden band. By contrast, smaller quantum dots (with radius between 2 and 3 nm) present relatively higher energy of the forbidden band, and, as such, emit light at shorter wavelengths, generating colors such as blue and green.

The inset of Fig. 4 shows the absorbance spectra of the GQDs, where the band with maxima wavelength at 330 nm is related to the n-π transition. The n-π transition may be associated with the presence of residual CO groups in the sample after the condensation reaction between ascorbic acid molecules [38].

The modified sensor was constructed after the preparation of the SPE with the fastening of the magnet and the synthesis of FG, GQDs and mag@MIP. To obtain the film (GQDs-FG-NF) on the SPE, an analysis was performed regarding the amount of FG in the proportions FG/solution of 0.5: 1.0, 1.0: 1.0, 1.5: 1.0 (mg/mL) and dispersion of GQDots in the GQDs/solution ratios of 0.33: 0.67, 0.66: 0.36, 0.99: 0.01 (mL/mL). The best peak current magnitude for EE2 was obtained under the application of 1.0 mg of FG, 990 μL of GQDs, and 10 μL of NF. NF is a perfluorosulfonated polymer, which was selected and employed in this study due to its great applicability when it comes to the development of electrochemical sensors [43]. This polymer is useful in the process involving the dispersion of materials in aqueous media and promotes better adhesion of the film on electrode surfaces, thus helping to improve the chemical and physical stability of the modified electrodes [43–45].

As can be seen in Fig. 5 , EE2 adsorption test was conducted for the mag@MIP and mag@MIP immobilized on the magnetic sensor (GQDs-FG-NF/SPE). To perform this test, different amounts of mag@MIP (2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 mg) and analyte adsorption time (1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 6.0 min) were evaluated using square-wave voltammetry (SWV) experiments (not shown). These experiments were carried out in a 5.0 mL electrochemical cell containing the supporting electrolyte and EE2. The best results were obtained under the application of 2.0 mg of the polymer (mag@MIP) with analyte adsorption time of 3.0 min (under stirring). These parameter conditions were chosen for the conduct of subsequent experiments.
The CV experiments were performed in order to study the electrochemical behavior of EE2 using the following electrodes: SPE, GR-NF/SPE, FG-NF/SPE, GQDs-FG-NF/SPE and (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE.

Fig. 6 shows the results obtained from the CV experiments. A comparison was made between the materials based on the cyclic voltammograms obtained for each material employed in the step by step SPE modification. All experiments were performed in 0.20 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (with pH 7.0) containing 5.0 μmol L−1 EE2. The results obtained show that the modification of SPE contributed to an increase in the anodic peak current (I pa) of the EE2. A comparison between the responses of the SPE and the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE showed a 10-fold increase in the I pa of EE2. This result demonstrates the contribution of mag@MIP toward the selective detection of the analyte. Moreover, the presence of FG and GQDs on the SPE surface helped to ensure an effective charge transfer from the EE2 to the transducer.

Analysis was carried out regarding the dependence of electrochemical response on pH using the pH range of 5.0–9.0. Fig. 7 shows the results obtained for SWV experiments performed in 2.0 μmol L−1 and EE2 0.2 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution. The inset data of ΔI pa vs. pH and ΔE p vs. pH provide information on the intensity of Ipa in relation to the pH applied and the linear relationship between the pH and the anodic peak potential (E pa). These data showed that the pH of 7.0 was the most suitable pH to be employed in subsequent steps of the work, since the application of this pH yielded the highest I pa intensity. Furthermore, an increase in pH was found to shift E p to less positive values, indicating the presence of protons in the electrooxidation of EE2. The slope (of −0.050 V pH−1) obtained was found to be close to the Nernstian theoretical value of −0.0592 V pH–1, which is typical for redox processes involving the same number of protons and electrons [46]. Scheme 1 presents the proposed electrochemical reaction for EE2 oxidation [47]; the electrochemical signal was monitored by SWV.

Scan rate analysis was conducted in the range of 10 mV s−1 to 90 mV s−1 for 2.0 mmol L−1 of EE2 in 0.20 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) using CV. As can be seen in Fig. S2A (Supplementary Material), an increase in the potential scan rate led to a linear increase in peak current.

Fig. S2A presents CV voltammograms at scan rate ranging from 10 to 90 mV s−1 used to construct the plots in the inset: (i) log I p vs. log v; (ii) I p vs. v and (iii) I p vs. v 1/2. The log I p vs. Iog v plots presented linear relationship with a slope of 1.01. As can be noted, the value of this slope is equal to the theoretical value of 1.0, which is typical for redox processes conducted by adsorption. This is in agreement with the graphs of I p vs. v and I p vs. v 1/2, which presented linear relationship with correlation coefficient (r) of 0.998 and 0.993, respectively.

Table S1 in the Supporting Material summarizes the parameters investigated for the optimization of square-wave adsorptive anodic stripping voltammetry (SWAdASV): amplitude (a), frequency (f), increase in potential (ΔE s), and the effect of pre-concentration (potential and time). The most suitable experimental conditions were obtained under the following: a = 50 mV, f = 30 Hz, ΔE s = 5 mV, pre-concentration potential = 0 V, and pre-concentration time = 75 s.

After the entire optimization process, EE2 was determined using the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor by the SWAdASV technique. Fig. 8 shows the analytical curves obtained under different EE2 concentrations. The analytical curve obtained presented linear response over the concentration range of 10 nmol L−1 to 2.5 μmol L−1, with limit of detection (LOD) of 2.6 nmol L−1, calculated using the equation: 3Sb/m, where Sb is the standard deviation of 10 blank solution measurements (n = 10) and m the slope of the analytical curve. The following equation was obtained for the analytical curve:

The data related to analytical performance for the determination of EE2 using the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor and other voltammetric sensors reported in the literature are shown in Table 1 [24,48–54]. With the exception of the methods reported by Nodehi et al. (2020) [50] and Prado et al. (2017) [51] – which presented comparable results, the proposed (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE is better than all other sensors reported in the literature in terms of detectability (lower LOD) and linear concentration range. Among the key advantages of the SPE modified with (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE include high stability, easy electrode construction, good repeatability, and low cost of fabrication, apart from the possibility of being used as a disposable device.

The statistical analysis of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor was conducted using 0.30 and 2.0 μmol L−1 concentrations of EE2 in 0.2 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (with pH 7.0). For this analysis, a sequence of electrochemical measurements was carried out using (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE (n = 10) and in different devices (n = 5), where the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.5% for intra-assay and 4.1% for inter-assay, respectively, were obtained. The RSD values obtained (RSD = (σ/μ) × 100%) show that the proposed analytical method involving the use of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor exhibits good precision, reproducibility and repeatability when it comes to EE2 detection. Where σ and μ are the standard deviation and the mean of the results, respectively.

The selectivity of the (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor was also evaluated in the absence and presence of some compounds typically present in biological fluids and/or environmental samples; these included Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, ascorbic acid, caffeine, dopamine, humic acid, urea and uric acid, and were employed in the ratio of 1:1 and 1:10 (analyte: possible interferent), respectively. The results obtained in the presence of these compounds showed relative standard deviation values ranging from −3.3 to 1.7% in all cases, as can be observed in Table S2 (Supplementary Material). Based on these results, one can conclude that the proposed method using SWV exhibited good selectivity for EE2 detection in the presence of the possible interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ethinylestradiol in river water, serum and urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the determination of EE2 in river water, serum and urine samples was analyzed through the application of the proposed (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor. The environmental (river water) and biological samples (serum and urine) were fortified and analyzed under two different concentrations of EE2 (0.080 and 1.0 μmol L−1). Table 2 shows the range of recovery rates obtained: from 96 to 105% for the environmental samples and between 97 and 104% for the biological samples. Thus, one can conclude that the proposed mechanism was satisfactory, considering that there was no matrix interference in the samples investigated.

The results obtained in the present study showed that the combination of GQDs and FG immobilized on the surface of SPE and the capture of the analyte by mag@MIP resulted in an increase in electrochemical signal, which led to the detection of EE2 in low concentration. The proposed (mag@MIP)-GQDs-FG-NF/SPE sensor has outstanding advantages including good stability, repeatability, reproducibility, low cost of fabrication, and relatively fast analysis with time < 30 min from the construction of analytical curve to the analysis of the sample, in addition to the possibility of being used as a disposable device. The electrochemical sensor was successfully applied in urine, serum and river water samples, where recovery rates of approximately 100% were obtained. The results obtained demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method and its promising applicability potential for the individual determination of this drug in different samples.

Anderson M. Santos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Ademar Wong: Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Thiago M. Prado: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Elson L. Fava: Methodology. Orlando Fatibello-Filho: Resources, Funding acquisition. Maria D.P.T. Sotomayor: Project administration, Supervision. Fernando C. Moraes: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001; PROJ. AUX/PE/PROEX Nº 0674/2018 CAPES; Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) process numbers 150184/2019-0, 405546/2018-1 and 408050/2018-7; and FAPESP process number 2017/10118-0, 2016/10012-4, 2019/00677-7 and 2020/01050-5.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121804.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.M. Prado, F.H. Cincotto, F.C. Moraes, S.A.S. Machado, Electrochemical Sensor-Based Ruthenium Nanoparticles on Reduced Graphene Oxide for the Simultaneous Determination of Ethinylestradiol and Amoxicillin, Electroanalysis 29 (2017) 1278-1285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Palladium nanoparticles/graphitic carbon Nitride nanosheets-carbon nanotubes as a catalytic amplification platform for the selective determination of 17α-ethinylestradiol in feedstuffs
Sci. Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.x. Zheng, M. Wang, X.z. Shi, C.m. Wang, Palladium Nanoparticles/Graphitic Carbon Nitride Nanosheets-Carbon Nanotubes as a Catalytic Amplification Platform for the Selective Determination of 17α-ethinylestradiol in Feedstuffs, Sci. Rep. 9 (2019) 14162.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric determination of 17α-ethinylestradiol hormone in supply dam using BDD electrode
J. Solid State Electrochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Perez, F.R. Simoes, L. Codognoto, Voltammetric determination of 17α-ethinylestradiol hormone in supply dam using BDD electrode, J. Solid State Electr. 20 (2016) 2471-2478.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric determination of ethinylestradiol at a carbon paste electrode in the presence of cetyl pyridine bromine
Bioelectrochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, Voltammetric determination of ethinylestradiol at a carbon paste electrode in the presence of cetyl pyridine bromine, Bioelectrochemistry 70 (2007) 263-268.
